text.title: python-async-patterns Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for python-async-patterns as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Calling sync I/O (requests.get, time.sleep, psycopg2.connect) inside async def.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Blocks the entire event loop for all concurrent tasks for the full duration of the call.

failure-mode.description: Calling a coroutine without await: result = fetch_data() instead of result = await fetch_data().
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: The coroutine is created but never executed; the call silently does nothing and returns a coroutine object.

failure-mode.description: Fire-and-forget with asyncio.create_task() without storing the reference.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Tasks without strong references may be garbage-collected mid-execution; exceptions are silently swallowed.

failure-mode.description: Catching Exception (or bare except) in async code, which includes CancelledError on Python 3.7; swallowing it prevents graceful shutdown.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: CancelledError must propagate for TaskGroup and timeout() to cancel correctly. On 3.8+ CancelledError no longer inherits Exception, but agents use old patterns.

failure-mode.description: Creating httpx.AsyncClient() inside a request handler or loop iteration.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Each instantiation opens a new connection pool; leaks sockets and breaks HTTP/2 keep-alive. App-scoped singleton is required.
